title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - LORA: LOW-RANK ADAPTATION OF LARGE LAN GUAGE MODELS

**Edward Hu** _[∗]_ **Yelong Shen** _[∗]_ **Phillip Wallis Zeyuan Allen-Zhu Yuanzhi Li Shean Wang Lu Wang Weizhu Chen** Microsoft Corporation edward.hu@mila.quebec _{_ yeshe, phwallis, zeyuana, swang, luw, wzchen _}_ @microsoft.com yuanzhil@andrew.cmu.edu

## ABSTRACT

An important paradigm of natural language processing consists of large-scale pretraining on general domain data and adaptation to particular tasks or domains. As we pre-train larger models, full fine-tuning, which retrains all model parameters, becomes less feasible. Using GPT-3 175B as an example – deploying independent instances of fine-tuned models, each with 175B parameters, is prohibitively expensive. We propose **Lo** w- **R** ank **A** daptation, or LoRA, which freezes the pre-trained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture, greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters for downstream tasks. Compared to GPT-3 175B fine-tuned with Adam, LoRA can reduce the number of trainable parameters by a factor of 10,000 and the GPU memory requirement by a factor of 3. LoRA performs on-par or better than finetuning in model quality on RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, and GPT-3, despite having fewer trainable parameters, a higher training throughput, and, unlike adapters, _no additional inference latency_ . We also provide an empirical investigation into rank-deficiency in language model adaptation, which sheds light on the efficacy of LoRA. We release a package that facilitates the integration of LoRA with PyTorch models and provide our implementations and model checkpoints for RoBERTa, DeBERTa, and GPT-2 at https://github.com/microsoft/LoRA.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Many applications in natural language processing rely on adapting _one_ large-scale, pre-trained language model to _multiple_ downstream applications. Such adaptation is usually done via _fine-tuning_ , which updates all the parameters of the pre-trained model. The major downside of fine-tuning is that the new model contains as many parameters as in the original model. As larger models are trained every few months, this changes from a mere “inconvenience” for GPT-2 (Radford et al., b) or RoBERTa large (Liu et al., 2019) to a critical deployment challenge for GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) with 175 billion trainable parameters.[1]

Many sought to mitigate this by adapting only some parameters or learning external modules for new tasks. This way, we only need to store and load a small number of task-specific parameters in addition to the pre-trained model for each task, greatly boosting the operational efficiency when deployed. However, existing techniques

bardzumyan et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021) (Section 3). More importantly, these method often fail to match the fine-tuning baselines, posing a trade-off between efficiency and model quality.

We take inspiration from Li et al. (2018a); Aghajanyan et al. (2020) which show that the learned over-parametrized models in fact reside on a low intrinsic dimension. We hypothesize that the change in weights during model adaptation also has a low “intrinsic rank”, leading to our proposed **Lo** w- **R** ank **A** daptation (LoRA) approach. LoRA allows us to train some dense layers in a neural network indirectly by optimizing rank decomposition matrices of the dense layers’ change during adaptation instead, while keeping the pre-trained weights frozen, as shown in Figure 1. Using GPT-3 175B as an example, we show that a very low rank (i.e., _r_ in Figure 1 can be one or two) suffices even when the full rank (i.e., _d_ ) is as high as 12,288, making LoRA both storage- and compute-efficient.

LoRA possesses several key advantages.

- A pre-trained model can be shared and used to build many small LoRA modules for different tasks. We can freeze the shared model and efficiently switch tasks by replacing the matrices _A_ and _B_ in Figure 1, reducing the storage requirement and task-switching overhead significantly.

- LoRA makes training more efficient and lowers the hardware barrier to entry by up to 3 times when using adaptive optimizers since we do not need to calculate the gradients or maintain the optimizer states for most parameters. Instead, we only optimize the injected, much smaller low-rank matrices.
- Our simple linear design allows us to merge the trainable matrices with the frozen weights when deployed, _introducing no inference latency_ compared to a fully fine-tuned model, by construction.
- LoRA is orthogonal to many prior methods and can be combined with many of them, such as prefix-tuning. We provide an example in Appendix H.

**Terminologies and Conventions** We make frequent references to the Transformer architecture and use the conventional terminologies for its dimensions. We call the input and output dimension size of a Transformer layer _dmodel_ . We use _Wq_ , _Wk_ , _Wv_ , and _Wo_ to refer to the query/key/value/output projection matrices in the self-attention module. _W_ or _W_ 0 refers to a pretrained weight matrix and ∆ _W_ its accumulated gradient update during adaptation. We use _r_ to denote the rank of a LoRA module. We follow the conventions set out by (Vaswani et al., 2017; Brown et al., 2020) and use Adam (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019; Kingma & Ba, 2017) for model optimization and use a Transformer MLP feedforward dimension _dffn_ = 4 _× dmodel_ .

## 2 PROBLEM STATEMENT

While our proposal is agnostic to training objective, we focus on conditional language modeling as our motivating use case. Below is a brief description of the language modeling problem and, in particular, the maximization of conditional probabilities given a task-specific prompt.

Suppose we are given a pre-trained autoregressive language model _P_ Φ( _y|x_ ) parametrized by Φ. For instance, _P_ Φ( _y|x_ ) can be a generic multi-task learner such as GPT (Radford et al., b; Brown et al., 2020) based on the Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017). Consider adapting this pre-trained model to downstream conditional text generation tasks, such as summarization, machine reading comprehension (MRC), and natural language to SQL (NL2SQL). Each downstream task is represented by a training dataset of context-target pairs: _Z_ = _{_ ( _xi, yi_ ) _}i_ =1 _,..,N_ , where both _xi_ and _yi_ are sequences of tokens. For example, in NL2SQL, _xi_ is a natural language query and _yi_ its corresponding SQL command; for summarization, _xi_ is the content of an article and _yi_ its summary.
During full fine-tuning, the model is initialized to pre-trained weights Φ0 and updated to Φ0 + ∆Φ by repeatedly following the gradient to maximize the conditional language modeling objective:

One of the main drawbacks for full fine-tuning is that for _each_ downstream task, we learn a _different_ set of parameters ∆Φ whose dimension _|_ ∆Φ _|_ equals _|_ Φ0 _|_ . Thus, if the pre-trained model is large (such as GPT-3 with _|_ Φ0 _| ≈_ 175 Billion), storing and deploying many independent instances of fine-tuned models can be challenging, if at all feasible.

In this paper, we adopt a more parameter-efficient approach, where the task-specific parameter increment ∆Φ = ∆Φ(Θ) is further encoded by a much smaller-sized set of parameters Θ with _|_ Θ _| ≪|_ Φ0 _|_ . The task of finding ∆Φ thus becomes optimizing over Θ:

In the subsequent sections, we propose to use a low-rank representation to encode ∆Φ that is both compute- and memory-efficient. When the pre-trained model is GPT-3 175B, the number of trainable parameters _|_ Θ _|_ can be as small as 0 _._ 01% of _|_ Φ0 _|_ .

## 3 AREN’T EXISTING SOLUTIONS GOOD ENOUGH?

The problem we set out to tackle is by no means new. Since the inception of transfer learning, dozens of works have sought to make model adaptation more parameter- and compute-efficient. See Section 6 for a survey of some of the well-known works. Using language modeling as an example, there are two prominent strategies when it comes to efficient adaptations: adding adapter layers (Houlsby et al., 2019; Rebuffi et al., 2017; Pfeiffer et al., 2021; R¨uckl´e et al., 2020) or optimizing some forms of the input layer activations (Li & Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Hambardzumyan et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). However, both strategies have their limitations, especially in a large-scale and latency-sensitive production scenario.

**Adapter Layers Introduce Inference Latency** There are many variants of adapters. We focus on the original design by Houlsby et al. (2019) which has two adapter layers per Transformer block and a more recent one by Lin et al. (2020) which has only one per block but with an additional LayerNorm (Ba et al., 2016). While one can reduce the overall latency by pruning layers or exploiting multi-task settings (R¨uckl´e et al., 2020; Pfeiffer et al., 2021), there is no direct ways to bypass the extra compute in adapter layers. This seems like a non-issue since adapter layers are designed to have few parameters (sometimes _<_ 1% of the original model) by having a small bottleneck dimension, which limits the FLOPs they can add. However, large neural networks rely on hardware parallelism to keep the latency low, and adapter layers have to be processed sequentially. This makes a difference in the online inference setting where the batch size is typically as small as one. In a generic scenario without model parallelism, such as running inference on GPT-2 (Radford et al., b) medium on a single GPU, we see a noticeable increase in latency when using adapters, even with a very small bottleneck dimension (Table 1).

The inference latency introduced by adapter layers can be significant in an online, short-sequence-length scenario. See the full study in Appendix C.

This problem gets worse when we need to shard the model as done in Shoeybi et al. (2020); Lepikhin et al. (2020), because the additional depth requires more synchronous GPU operations such as AllReduce and Broadcast, unless we store the adapter parameters redundantly many times.

**Directly Optimizing the Prompt is Hard** The other direction, as exemplified by prefix tuning (Li & Liang, 2021), faces a different challenge. We observe that prefix tuning is difficult to optimize and that its performance changes non-monotonically in trainable parameters, confirming similar observations in the original paper. More fundamentally, reserving a part of the sequence length for adaptation necessarily reduces the sequence length available to process a downstream task, which we suspect makes tuning the prompt underperform other methods. We defer the study on task performance to Section 5.

## 4 OUR METHOD

We describe the simple design of LoRA and its practical benefits. The principles outlined here apply to any dense layers in deep learning models, though we only focus on certain weights in Transformer language models in our experiments as the motivating use case.

## 4.1 LOW-RANK-PARAMETRIZED UPDATE MATRICES

A neural network contains many dense layers which perform matrix multiplication. The weight matrices in these layers typically have full ranks. When adapting to a specific task, Aghajanyan et al. (2020) shows that the pre-trained language models have a low “instrisic dimension” and can still learn efficiently despite a random projection to a smaller subspace. Inspired by this, we hypothesize the updates to the weights also have a low “intrinsic rank” during adaptation. For a pre-trained weight matrix _W_ 0 _∈_ R _[d][×][k]_ , we constrain its update by representing the latter with a low-rank decomposition _W_ 0 + ∆ _W_ = _W_ 0 + _BA_ , where _B ∈_ R _[d][×][r] , A ∈_ R _[r][×][k]_ , and the rank _r ≪_ min( _d, k_ ). During training, _W_ 0 is frozen and does not receive gradient updates, while _A_ and _B_ contain trainable parameters. Note both _W_ 0 and ∆ _W_ = _BA_ are multiplied with the same input, and their respective output vectors are summed coordinate-wise. For _h_ = _W_ 0 _x_ , our modified forward pass yields:

We illustrate our reparametrization in Figure 1. We use a random Gaussian initialization for _A_ and zero for _B_ , so ∆ _W_ = _BA_ is zero at the beginning of training. We then scale ∆ _Wx_ by _[α] r_[, where] _[ α]_ is a constant in _r_ . When optimizing with Adam, tuning _α_ is roughly the same as tuning the learning rate if we scale the initialization appropriately. As a result, we simply set _α_ to the first _r_ we try and do not tune it. This scaling helps to reduce the need to retune hyperparameters when we vary _r_ (Yang & Hu, 2021).

**A Generalization of Full Fine-tuning.** A more general form of fine-tuning allows the training of a subset of the pre-trained parameters. LoRA takes a step further and does not require the accumulated gradient update to weight matrices to have full ranks during adaptation. This means that when applying LoRA to all weight matrices and training all biases[2] , we roughly recover the expressiveness of full fine-tuning by setting the LoRA rank _r_ to the rank of the pre-trained weight matrices. In other words, as we increase the number of trainable parameters[3] , training LoRA roughly converges to training the original model, while adapter-based methods converges to an MLP and prefix-based methods to a model that cannot take long input sequences.

**No Additional Inference Latency.** When deployed in production, we can explicitly compute and store _W_ = _W_ 0 + _BA_ and perform inference as usual. Note that both _W_ 0 and _BA_ are in R _[d][×][k]_ . When we need to switch to another downstream task, we can recover _W_ 0 by subtracting _BA_ and then adding a different _B[′] A[′]_ , a quick operation with very little memory overhead. Critically, this guarantees that we do not introduce any additional latency during inference compared to a fine-tuned model by construction.

## 4.2 APPLYING LORA TO TRANSFORMER

In principle, we can apply LoRA to any subset of weight matrices in a neural network to reduce the number of trainable parameters. In the Transformer architecture, there are four weight matrices in

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

the self-attention module ( _Wq, Wk, Wv, Wo_ ) and two in the MLP module. We treat _Wq_ (or _Wk_ , _Wv_ ) as a single matrix of dimension _dmodel × dmodel_ , even though the output dimension is usually sliced into attention heads. We limit our study to **only adapting the attention weights** for downstream tasks and freeze the MLP modules (so they are not trained in downstream tasks) both for simplicity and parameter-efficiency.We further study the effect on adapting different types of attention weight matrices in a Transformer in Section B.1. We leave the empirical investigation of adapting the MLP layers, LayerNorm layers, and biases to a future work.

**Practical Benefits and Limitations.** The most significant benefit comes from the reduction in memory and storage usage. For a large Transformer trained with Adam, we reduce that VRAM usage by up to 2 _/_ 3 if _r ≪ dmodel_ as we do not need to store the optimizer states for the frozen parameters. On GPT-3 175B, we reduce the VRAM consumption during training from 1.2TB to 350GB. With _r_ = 4 and only the query and value projection matrices being adapted, the checkpoint size is reduced by roughly 10,000 _×_ (from 350GB to 35MB)[4] . This allows us to train with significantly fewer GPUs and avoid I/O bottlenecks. Another benefit is that we can switch between tasks while deployed at a much lower cost by only swapping the LoRA weights as opposed to all the parameters. This allows for the creation of many customized models that can be swapped in and out on the fly on machines that store the pre-trained weights in VRAM. We also observe a 25% speedup during training on GPT-3 175B compared to full fine-tuning[5] as we do not need to calculate the gradient for the vast majority of the parameters. See Appendix L for how to calculate gradient when using LoRA.

LoRA also has its limitations. For example, it is not straightforward to batch inputs to different tasks with different _A_ and _B_ in a single forward pass, if one chooses to absorb _A_ and _B_ into _W_ to eliminate additional inference latency. Though it is possible to not merge the weights and dynamically choose the LoRA modules to use for samples in a batch for scenarios where latency is not critical.

## 5 EMPIRICAL EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the downstream task performance of LoRA on RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), DeBERTa (He et al., 2021), and GPT-2 (Radford et al., b), before scaling up to GPT-3 175B (Brown et al., 2020). Our experiments cover a wide range of tasks, from natural language understanding (NLU) to generation (NLG). Specifically, we evaluate on the GLUE (Wang et al., 2019) benchmark for RoBERTa and DeBERTa. We follow the setup of Li & Liang (2021) on GPT-2 for a direct comparison and add WikiSQL (Zhong et al., 2017) (NL to SQL queries) and SAMSum (Gliwa et al., 2019) (conversation summarization) for large-scale experiments on GPT-3. See Appendix D for more details on the datasets we use. We use NVIDIA Tesla V100 for all experiments.

## 5.1 BASELINES

To compare with other baselines broadly, we replicate the setups used by prior work and reuse their reported numbers whenever possible. This, however, means that some baselines might only appear in certain experiments. We point out that for most tasks, our goal is not to compete with SOTA because 1) for tasks such as WikiSQL, SOTA uses specialized SQL-aware decoders, and; 2) SOTA makes frequent use of techniques such as ensembling and adversarial training, which might confound our study on adaptation. Thus, we focus on a scenario where we are given a powerful pre-trained autoregressive language model and use as few task-specific modifications as possible. See Appendix E for how we count the number of trainable parameters for different baselines.

**Fine-Tuning (FT)** is a common approach for adaptation. During fine-tuning, the model is initialized to the pre-trained weights and biases, and all model parameters undergo gradient updates.A simple variant is to update only some layers while freezing others. We include one such baseline reported in prior work (Li & Liang, 2021) on GPT-2, which adapts just the last two layers ( **FT[Top2]** ).

**Bias-only or BitFit** is a baseline where we only train the bias vectors while freezing everything else. Contemporarily, this baseline has also been studied by BitFit (Zaken et al., 2021).

**Prefix-embedding tuning (PreEmbed)** inserts special tokens among the input tokens. These special tokens have trainable word embeddings and are generally not in the model’s vocabulary. Where to place such tokens can have an impact on performance. We focus on “prefixing”, which prepends such tokens to the prompt, and “infixing”, which appends to the prompt; both are discussed in Li & Liang (2021).

**Prefix-layer tuning (PreLayer)** is an extension to prefix-embedding tuning. Instead of just learning the word embeddings (or equivalently, the activations after the embedding layer) for some special tokens, we learn the activations after every Transformer layer. The activations computed from previous layers are simply replaced by trainable ones.

**Adapter tuning** as proposed in Houlsby et al. (2019) inserts adapter layers between the selfattention module (and the MLP module) and the subsequent residual connection. There are two fully connected layers with biases in an adapter layer with a nonlinearity in between. We call this original design **Adapter[H]** . Recently, Lin et al. (2020) proposed a more efficient design with the adapter layer applied only after the MLP module and after a LayerNorm. We call it **Adapter[L]** . This is very similar to another deign proposed in Pfeiffer et al. (2021), which we call **Adapter[P]** . We also include another baseline call AdapterDrop (R¨uckl´e et al., 2020) which drops some adapter layers for greater efficiency ( **Adapter[D]** ). We cite numbers from prior works whenever possible to maximize the number of baselines we compare with; they are in rows with an asterisk (*) in the first column.

**LoRA** adds trainable pairs of rank decomposition matrices in parallel to existing weight matrices. As mentioned in Section 4.2, we only apply LoRA to _Wq_ and _Wv_ in most experiments for simplicity.

## 5.2 ROBERTA BASE/LARGE

RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) optimized the pre-training recipe originally proposed in BERT (Devlin et al., 2019a) and boosted the latter’s task performance without introducing many more trainable parameters. While RoBERTa has been overtaken by much larger models on NLP leaderboards such as the GLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2019) in recent years, it remains a competitive and popular pre-trained model for its size among practitioners. We take the pre-trained RoBERTa base (125M) and RoBERTa large (355M) from the HuggingFace Transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020)

## 5.3 DEBERTA XXL

DeBERTa (He et al., 2021) is a more recent variant of BERT that is trained on a much larger scale and performs very competitively on benchmarks such as GLUE (Wang et al., 2019) and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2020). We evaluate if LoRA can still match the performance of a fully fine-tuned DeBERTa XXL (1.5B) on GLUE. The result is presented in Table 2 (Bottom Section). See Section F.2 for details on the hyperparameters used.

## 5.4 GPT-2 MEDIUM/LARGE

Having shown that LoRA can be a competitive alternative to full fine-tuning on NLU, we hope to answer if LoRA still prevails on NLG models, such as GPT-2 medium and large (Radford et al., b). We keep our setup as close as possible to Li & Liang (2021) for a direct comparison. Due to space constraint, we only present our result on E2E NLG Challenge (Table 3) in this section. See Section I.1 for results on WebNLG (Gardent et al., 2017) and DART (Nan et al., 2020). We include a list of the hyperparameters used in Section F.3.

## 5.5 SCALING UP TO GPT-3 175B

As a final stress test for LoRA, we scale up to GPT-3 with 175 billion parameters. Due to the high training cost, we only report the typical standard deviation for a given task over random seeds, as opposed to providing one for every entry. See Section F.4 for details on the hyperparameters used.

As shown in Table 4, LoRA matches or exceeds the fine-tuning baseline on all three datasets. Note that not all methods benefit monotonically from having more trainable parameters, as shown in Figure 2. We observe a significant performance drop when we use more than 256 special tokens for prefix-embedding tuning or more than 32 special tokens for prefix-layer tuning. This corroborates

## 6 RELATED WORKS

**Transformer Language Models.** Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) is a sequence-to-sequence architecture that makes heavy use of self-attention. Radford et al. (a) applied it to autoregressive language modeling by using a stack of Transformer decoders. Since then, Transformer-based language models have dominated NLP, achieving the state-of-the-art in many tasks. A new paradigm emerged with BERT (Devlin et al., 2019b) and GPT-2 (Radford et al., b) – both are large Transformer language models trained on a large amount of text – where fine-tuning on task-specific data after pretraining on general domain data provides a significant performance gain compared to training on task-specific data directly. Training larger Transformers generally results in better performance and remains an active research direction. GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) is the largest single Transformer language model trained to-date with 175B parameters.

**Prompt Engineering and Fine-Tuning.** While GPT-3 175B can adapt its behavior with just a few additional training examples, the result depends heavily on the input prompt (Brown et al., 2020). This necessitates an empirical art of composing and formatting the prompt to maximize a model’s performance on a desired task, which is known as prompt engineering or prompt hacking.

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Fine-tuning retrains a model pre-trained on general domains to a specific task Devlin et al. (2019b); Radford et al. (a). Variants of it include learning just a subset of the parameters Devlin et al. (2019b); Collobert & Weston (2008), yet practitioners often retrain all of them to maximize the downstream performance. However, the enormity of GPT-3 175B makes it challenging to perform fine-tuning in the usual way due to the large checkpoint it produces and the high hardware barrier to entry since it has the same memory footprint as pre-training.

**Parameter-Efficient Adaptation.** Many have proposed inserting _adapter_ layers between existing layers in a neural network (Houlsby et al., 2019; Rebuffi et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2020). Our method uses a similar bottleneck structure to impose a low-rank constraint on the weight updates. The key functional difference is that our learned weights can be merged with the main weights during inference, thus not introducing any latency, which is not the case for the adapter layers (Section 3). A comtenporary extension of adapter is COMPACTER (Mahabadi et al., 2021), which essentially parametrizes the adapter layers using Kronecker products with some predetermined weight sharing scheme. Similarly, combining LoRA with other tensor product-based methods could potentially improve its parameter efficiency, which we leave to future work. More recently, many proposed optimizing the input word embeddings in lieu of fine-tuning, akin to a continuous and differentiable generalization of prompt engineering (Li & Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Hambardzumyan et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). We include comparisons with Li & Liang (2021) in our experiment section. However, this line of works can only scale up by using more special tokens in the prompt, which take up available sequence length for task tokens when positional embeddings are learned.

**Low-Rank Structures in Deep Learning.** Low-rank structure is very common in machine learning. A lot of machine learning problems have certain intrinsic low-rank structure (Li et al., 2016; Cai et al., 2010; Li et al., 2018b; Grasedyck et al., 2013). Moreover, it is known that for many deep learning tasks, a heavily over-parametrized neural network will exhibit low-rank properties after training (Oymak et al., 2019). Some prior works even explicitly impose the low-rank constraint when training the original neural network (Sainath et al., 2013; Povey et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2014; Jaderberg et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2016; Khodak et al., 2021; Denil et al., 2014); however, to the best of our knowledge, none of these works considers low-rank update to a frozen model for _adaptation to downstream tasks_ . In theory literature, it is known that neural networks outperform other classical learning methods, including the corresponding (finite-width) neural tangent kernels (Allen-Zhu et al., 2019; Li & Liang, 2018) when the underlying concept class has certain low-rank structure (Ghorbani et al., 2020; Allen-Zhu & Li, 2019; Allen-Zhu & Li, 2020a). Another theory result in Allen-Zhu & Li (2020b) suggests that low-rank adaptations can be useful for adversarial training. In sum, we believe that our proposed low-rank adaptation update is well-motivated by the literature.

## 7 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK

Fine-tuning enormous language models is prohibitively expensive in terms of the hardware required and the storage/switching cost for hosting independent instances for different tasks. We propose LoRA, an efficient adaptation strategy that neither introduces inference latency nor reduces input sequence length while retaining high model quality. Importantly, it allows for quick task-switching when deployed as a service by sharing the vast majority of the model parameters. While we focused on Transformer language models, the proposed principles are generally applicable to any neural networks with dense layers.

There are many directions for future works. 1) LoRA can be combined with other efficient adaptation methods, potentially providing orthogonal improvement. 2) The mechanism behind fine-tuning or LoRA is far from clear – how are features learned during pre-training transformed to do well on downstream tasks? We believe that LoRA makes it more tractable to answer this than full finetuning. 3) We mostly depend on heuristics to select the weight matrices to apply LoRA to. Are there more principled ways to do it? 4) Finally, the rank-deficiency of ∆ _W_ suggests that _W_ could be rank-deficient as well, which can inspire future works.

9

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), a technique for fine-tuning large language models by updating only a small set of low-rank decomposition matrices while keeping the original weights frozen. The authors demonstrate that LoRA achieves performance parity with full fine-tuning on models like GPT-3 175B while drastically reducing memory requirements and eliminating inference latency through weight merging.

The paper presents a highly significant and practical contribution to the field of parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Its primary strength lies in the elegant mathematical formulation that allows for zero-latency inference, a major advantage over previous adapter-based methods. The empirical results across various scales (RoBERTa to GPT-3) are compelling and suggest a fundamental property of how large models adapt to new tasks.

Overview issues:

- Unclear Heuristics for Weight Matrix Selection: In Section 4.2, the authors state they limit their study to adapting only attention weights (Wq, Wk, Wv, Wo) and freeze the MLP modules for simplicity. However, the paper lacks a systematic ablation or theoretical justification for why the MLP layers—which contain a significant portion of a Transformer's parameters—are excluded from the adaptation process. Since MLP layers often capture factual knowledge, their omission might limit the model's ability to adapt to domain-specific knowledge-intensive tasks. A more thorough comparison in Section 5 or Appendix B showing the trade-offs of including MLP layers versus increasing the rank 'r' in attention layers would strengthen the methodological claims.

- Lack of Principled Guidance for Rank Selection: The paper demonstrates in Section 7.1 and Figure 2 that a very low rank (r=1, 2, or 4) often suffices for complex tasks, but it does not provide a methodology for determining the optimal rank 'r' for a new, unseen task. The current approach relies on empirical search, which partially offsets the 'efficiency' gains if many training runs are needed to find the right hyperparameter. The authors should investigate whether the 'intrinsic rank' of a task can be estimated a priori or if a dynamic rank allocation strategy could be employed to avoid manual tuning.

- Limited Analysis of Multi-Task Batching Constraints: Section 4.2 mentions a limitation regarding the difficulty of batching inputs for different tasks when LoRA weights are merged into the backbone. While the paper claims no inference latency, this is only true if the hardware is dedicated to a single task or if the weights are un-merged and re-merged frequently. The paper lacks a quantitative analysis of the overhead involved in swapping LoRA modules in a high-throughput production environment. Including a 'time-to-swap' benchmark or a discussion on the memory bandwidth costs of maintaining multiple LoRA adapters in VRAM would provide a more realistic picture of deployment feasibility.

- Insufficient Theoretical Grounding for Rank-Deficiency: While Section 7 provides an empirical investigation into the rank-deficiency of the weight updates, the paper stops short of explaining *why* the updates are low-rank. The hypothesis that the change in weights resides on a low intrinsic dimension is supported by results, but the connection to the pre-training objective or the geometry of the loss landscape is not explored. The paper would be more impactful if it linked the success of LoRA to existing theoretical work on over-parameterization or neural tangent kernels to explain why a rank of 4 can effectively steer a 175B parameter model.

- Missing Interaction Analysis with Quantization: The authors highlight GPU memory savings in Section 4.2, but they do not explore how LoRA interacts with other common compression techniques like 4-bit or 8-bit quantization. Since the primary audience for LoRA is researchers with limited hardware, understanding if the low-rank matrices are sensitive to the precision of the frozen backbone is vital. A small experiment in Section 5 showing LoRA performance on a quantized GPT-2 or RoBERTa model would clarify if these efficiency gains are additive or if quantization degrades the low-rank signal.

- Missing verification of the weight merging and un-merging process: The paper claims LoRA introduces zero inference latency because the low-rank matrices can be merged with the frozen weights. However, it fails to demonstrate that this process is numerically stable or reversible in practice. If repeated merging and un-merging for different tasks leads to floating-point drift or accumulation of rounding errors, the 'no latency' claim is compromised by a need for periodic weight resets. The authors should provide a small numerical experiment showing the weight difference ||W_original - (W_merged - BA)|| after several iterations of switching between different task adapters to ensure the backbone remains pristine.

- Absence of wall-clock training time benchmarks for GPT-3: The abstract and introduction emphasize that LoRA makes training more efficient and reduces the hardware barrier to entry. While the paper provides parameter counts and VRAM savings, it lacks concrete wall-clock training time comparisons for the 175B model. Readers cannot calibrate whether the 25% speedup mentioned in Section 4.2 translates to significant real-world savings without a table comparing total training hours for a standard benchmark like WikiSQL. A table showing the time-per-epoch for full fine-tuning versus LoRA on a fixed hardware cluster (e.g., 8x A100) is necessary to support the efficiency claims.

- Lack of comparison with simple partial fine-tuning baselines: The paper positions LoRA against adapters and prefix tuning but misses a critical comparison to simpler parameter-efficient baselines like fine-tuning only the LayerNorm parameters or only the top-k layers. Since LoRA requires a specific reparameterization, it is important to show that it outperforms these 'zero-code-change' methods. The authors should include a baseline in Table 2 or Table 4 that adapts only the LayerNorm layers (as in BitFit but for normalization) to prove that the low-rank structure is actually superior to just selecting a different subset of existing parameters.

- Missing discussion on the initialization of the alpha scaling factor: Section 4.1 introduces a scaling factor alpha/r and states that alpha is set to the first rank tried and not tuned. This is a significant implementation detail that lacks empirical or theoretical justification. It is unclear if the performance of the model is sensitive to this initial choice of alpha or if it interacts with the learning rate in a way that could lead to divergence on new architectures. The paper should include a small sensitivity plot for alpha (e.g., for GPT-2 on E2E) to demonstrate that the 'no-tuning' rule is a reliable heuristic for practitioners.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a systematic ablation study comparing the adaptation of MLP layers versus attention layers to justify the current focus.

- Provide a more detailed analysis of the 'swap overhead' for LoRA modules in multi-task inference scenarios to clarify deployment limitations.

- Add a discussion or preliminary experiment on the interaction between LoRA and model quantization (e.g., FP16 vs INT8) to address consumer-grade hardware constraints.

- Expand the empirical investigation of rank 'r' to include a heuristic or 'rule of thumb' for practitioners to select the rank based on task complexity or dataset size.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter counts for Adapter variants in Table 1
comments.explanation: Table 1 shows a parameter count (|Θ|) of 0.5M for the first column but 11M for the others. It is unclear why the parameter count for the same model architecture (GPT-2 medium) would change so drastically across columns unless the bottleneck dimension was varied specifically for different batch sizes. This is not standard practice for a latency benchmark. The table header or the |Θ| row should clarify if these counts are fixed properties of the Adapter variants or if they were tuned per-column, which would confound the latency comparison.

comments.title: Inaccurate rank requirement for full fine-tuning expressiveness
comments.quote: This means that when applying LoRA to all weight matrices and training all biases, we roughly recover the expressiveness of full fine-tuning by setting the LoRA rank $r$ to the rank of the pre-trained weight matrices.
comments.explanation: The claim that expressiveness is recovered by setting $r$ to the rank of $W_0$ is mathematically imprecise. In full fine-tuning, the update $\Delta W$ is an arbitrary matrix that can have a rank up to $\min(d, k)$. The rank of the pre-trained matrix $W_0$ does not bound the rank of the optimal update required for a new task. To truly recover the hypothesis space of full fine-tuning, $r$ must be able to reach $\min(d, k)$, regardless of $W_0$'s properties. The text should state that expressiveness is recovered as $r$ approaches the maximum possible rank of the weight matrix.

comments.title: Inconsistency in LoRA performance scaling on SAMSum
comments.explanation: Table 4 shows a performance regression for LoRA on the SAMSum dataset as trainable parameters increase from 4.7M to 37.7M. Specifically, the R1/R2/RL scores drop from 53.8/29.8/45.9 to 53.4/29.2/45.1. This contradicts the general claim that LoRA exhibits better scalability, as the larger configuration performs worse than the smaller one here. The analysis should acknowledge that LoRA performance can saturate or degrade on certain tasks when increasing rank, similar to the observations made for prefix-tuning.

comments.title: Inconsistent Average Calculation for RoBbase (LoRA)
comments.explanation: The average score for RoBbase (LoRA) in Table 2 is inconsistent with the individual task scores. Summing the eight scores (87.5, 95.1, 89.7, 63.4, 93.3, 90.8, 86.6, 91.5) yields 697.9, which results in an arithmetic mean of 87.2375. While this rounds to 87.2, the RoBbase (FT) row sums to 691.4 for an average of 86.425, which is also rounded to 86.4. The rounding convention is inconsistent across the column; the authors should use a standard rule (e.g., always rounding to the nearest tenth) for all entries.

comments.title: Logical gap in rank-deficiency implication
comments.quote: 4) Finally, the rank-deficiency of $\Delta W$ suggests that $W$ could be rank-deficient as well, which can inspire future works.
comments.explanation: The suggestion that the rank-deficiency of the update matrix $\Delta W$ implies the pre-trained weight matrix $W$ might also be rank-deficient is not necessarily supported by the results. A small update can reside in a low-dimensional subspace even if the original matrix $W$ is full-rank. Many optimization theories distinguish between the rank of the base signal and the rank of the perturbation. Without a singular value decomposition (SVD) analysis of the original $W$, this conjecture remains speculative.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The LoRA paper presents a parameter-efficient adaptation method for large language models using low-rank matrix decomposition. While the empirical results are strong and the method is influential, the paper has several significant methodological gaps that limit its scientific contribution. The main concerns are the lack of theoretical justification for the low-rank assumption, insufficient ablation studies on rank selection, limited exploration of which layers to adapt, and incomplete analysis of deployment trade-offs.

The paper introduces a practical and impactful method for efficient fine-tuning that has been widely adopted. The empirical evaluation across multiple model families and tasks is comprehensive, and the performance gains over adapter-based methods are convincing. However, the scientific contribution is weakened by the absence of theoretical grounding and several methodological shortcuts that leave important questions unanswered.

Overview issues:

- Missing Theoretical Justification for Low-Rank Assumption: The paper's core hypothesis—that weight updates during adaptation have low intrinsic rank—lacks formal theoretical justification. While the authors cite Aghajanyan et al. (2020) showing pre-trained models have low intrinsic dimension, they provide no theoretical analysis of why the *updates* ΔW should be low-rank. This gap is significant because the entire method rests on this assumption. The paper would be strengthened by either a theoretical derivation showing why adaptation updates are rank-deficient, or at least an empirical analysis of the singular value spectrum of actual gradient updates during fine-tuning across different tasks and model scales.

- Insufficient Rank Selection Analysis: The paper provides minimal guidance on selecting the rank hyperparameter r, which is crucial for practical application. While Figure 2 shows performance vs. parameter count, it doesn't systematically analyze how performance varies with rank for different tasks and model sizes. The authors claim r=1 or 2 suffices for GPT-3 but don't investigate whether this holds across diverse tasks or why. A proper ablation should show performance curves for multiple ranks (e.g., r=1,2,4,8,16) across different model families and tasks, along with analysis of the rank-performance trade-off. Without this, practitioners lack principled guidance for rank selection.

- Limited Exploration of Layer-wise Adaptation Strategy: The paper arbitrarily chooses to adapt only W_q and W_v matrices while freezing MLP layers, citing "simplicity and parameter-efficiency" as the reason (Section 4.2). This is a significant methodological decision that deserves thorough investigation. The authors should provide ablation studies comparing: (1) adapting only attention weights vs only MLP weights vs both; (2) adapting different attention matrices (W_q, W_k, W_v, W_o); (3) layer-wise analysis of which layers benefit most from LoRA. Without this analysis, it's unclear whether the chosen strategy is optimal or just one of many possible configurations.

- Incomplete Analysis of Deployment Trade-offs: The paper claims "no additional inference latency" but provides an incomplete analysis of real-world deployment costs. While Table 1 shows adapter latency for small batch sizes, it doesn't analyze the computational overhead of dynamic task switching with LoRA modules. The authors mention it's "not straightforward to batch inputs to different tasks" when weights are merged (Section 4.2), but don't quantify this limitation. A thorough analysis should include: (1) latency comparison for dynamic task switching scenarios; (2) memory overhead when storing multiple LoRA modules; (3) computational cost of weight merging/unmerging operations. This analysis is crucial for understanding LoRA's practical advantages in production systems.

- Lack of Multi-task Interference Analysis: The paper emphasizes LoRA's ability to support multiple task-specific modules but doesn't investigate potential interference between them. When multiple LoRA modules are trained on related tasks, do their weight updates interfere or transfer knowledge? The authors should provide experiments showing: (1) performance when training multiple LoRA modules sequentially on the same base model; (2) analysis of weight overlap between different task adapters; (3) negative transfer scenarios. This analysis is important for understanding LoRA's behavior in multi-task settings, which is a key claimed advantage.

- Missing demonstration of low-rank structure: The paper's central claim that weight updates during adaptation have low intrinsic rank lacks empirical verification. While the authors cite prior work on intrinsic dimensionality of pre-trained models, they never actually demonstrate that the ΔW matrices obtained during fine-tuning are rank-deficient. A complete paper should include an analysis of the singular value spectrum of actual weight updates from full fine-tuning experiments, showing how quickly the singular values decay for different tasks and model scales. This demonstration would provide concrete evidence supporting the low-rank assumption that underpins the entire LoRA approach.

- No worked example of LoRA computation: The paper presents the LoRA formulation abstractly but never walks through a concrete computation. Readers would benefit from a detailed worked example showing how LoRA operates on a specific Transformer layer. For instance, the authors could compute the effective weight matrix for a GPT-2 medium attention layer with d_model=1024 and rank r=8, showing the dimensions of A and B matrices, the parameter count reduction, and how the forward pass computation changes. This concrete example would help readers understand the practical implementation and parameter savings.

- Missing simulation of task-switching scenario: The paper claims LoRA enables efficient task-switching by swapping small adapter matrices, but provides no experimental demonstration of this key advantage. A complete evaluation should include a simulation of a multi-task deployment scenario, measuring: (1) time to switch between tasks by loading different LoRA weights, (2) memory footprint when storing multiple LoRA modules, and (3) performance when sequentially training multiple LoRA adapters on the same base model. Without this demonstration, the claimed operational efficiency advantage remains theoretical.

- Insufficient analysis of rank-performance relationship: While Figure 2 shows performance vs parameter count, the paper lacks a systematic analysis of how rank selection affects performance across different tasks and model sizes. The authors claim r=1 or 2 suffices for GPT-3 but don't investigate whether this generalizes. A proper ablation should include performance curves for multiple ranks (e.g., r=1,2,4,8,16) across different model families and tasks, along with analysis of when higher ranks become necessary. This analysis is crucial for providing practitioners with principled guidance on rank selection.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add theoretical analysis or empirical evidence justifying the low-rank assumption for weight updates during adaptation

- Include comprehensive ablation studies on rank selection across different model sizes and tasks with performance curves

- Provide systematic analysis of which layers and weight matrices to adapt, comparing different adaptation strategies

- Conduct detailed deployment analysis including dynamic task switching costs and multi-task interference experiments

- Add analysis of the singular value spectrum of actual fine-tuning updates to empirically validate the rank-deficiency claim

comments.title: Incorrect rank recovery claim
comments.quote: In other words, as we increase the number of trainable parameters , training LoRA roughly converges to training the original model, while adapter-based methods converges to an MLP and prefix-based methods to a model that cannot take long input sequences.
comments.explanation: The claim that LoRA converges to full fine-tuning when rank equals pre-trained weight matrix rank is incorrect. If $W_0$ has rank $R$ and we set LoRA rank $r=R$, the update $\Delta W = BA$ can only span an $R$-dimensional subspace. However, full fine-tuning allows updates in the full $d \times k$ parameter space. Even if $W_0$ has rank $R < \min(d,k)$, full fine-tuning can modify $W_0$ in directions outside its original row/column space, which rank-$R$ LoRA cannot achieve. Rewrite the quoted text as "In other words, as we increase the number of trainable parameters, training LoRA with rank $r$ set to the rank of the pre-trained weight matrices can represent any update within the same subspace as $W_0$, while adapter-based methods converge to an MLP and prefix-based methods to a model that cannot take long input sequences" because the original claim overstates the equivalence to full fine-tuning.

comments.title: Table 1 parameter count inconsistency
comments.explanation: The table shows inconsistent parameter counts across the three experimental configurations. The header row lists 0.5M, 11M, and 11M trainable parameters, but this doesn't align with the experimental setup. If AdapterL and AdapterH are different adapter variants as stated, they should have different parameter counts, yet both columns show 11M. More critically, the Fine-Tune/LoRA row should have 0 parameters for LoRA (since it freezes the base model) or full model parameters for fine-tuning, but the table doesn't distinguish between these fundamentally different approaches. The parameter count column appears to be mislabeled or incorrectly filled, making it impossible to interpret the relationship between parameter count and latency. Rewrite the table to clearly distinguish between the three methods (Fine-Tune, LoRA, AdapterL, AdapterH) and show their respective parameter counts, or remove the parameter count column if it's not essential for the latency comparison.

comments.title: VRAM and checkpoint size inconsistency
comments.quote: On GPT-3 175B, we reduce the VRAM consumption during training from 1.2TB to 350GB. With $r=4$ and only the query and value projection matrices being adapted, the checkpoint size is reduced by roughly 10,000$\times$ (from 350GB to 35MB).
comments.explanation: The numbers create a logical inconsistency. VRAM reduction from 1.2TB to 350GB is a 3.4× reduction, but the checkpoint reduction claim uses 350GB as the baseline. Since checkpoint size (model weights only) should be smaller than VRAM usage (weights + optimizer states + activations), the baseline checkpoint cannot be 350GB when VRAM is already reduced to 350GB. The math 350GB/35MB = 10,000× is correct, but the baseline reference is wrong. Rewrite the passage as "On GPT-3 175B, we reduce the VRAM consumption during training from 1.2TB to 350GB. With $r=4$ and only the query and value projection matrices being adapted, the checkpoint size is reduced by roughly 10,000$\times$ (from the original 350GB checkpoint to 35MB)" because the baseline checkpoint size should be referenced to the original model, not the reduced VRAM usage.

comments.title: Table 3 contains duplicate method entries
comments.explanation: Table 3 lists two identical entries for 'GPT-2 M (AdapterL)*' with different parameter counts (0.37M vs 11.09M) and different performance metrics. This appears to be a copy-paste error where one entry should have been labeled differently, likely 'AdapterH' for the 11.09M parameter version to match the pattern seen in the GPT-2 L section where AdapterL and AdapterH are distinguished. The duplicate labeling creates confusion about which results correspond to which adapter configuration. Rewrite the second 'GPT-2 M (AdapterL)*' entry as 'GPT-2 M (AdapterH)*' to maintain consistency with the naming convention used elsewhere in the table.

comments.title: Inconsistent adapter method labeling in Table 3
comments.explanation: Table 3 shows two entries for 'GPT-2 L (AdapterL)' with different parameter counts (0.88M vs 23.00M) but no distinction in the method name. This is inconsistent with the GPT-2 M section where different parameter counts correspond to different adapter variants (AdapterL vs AdapterH). The 23.00M parameter version should likely be labeled 'AdapterH' to match the pattern established in the GPT-2 M section and maintain consistency across the table. Without this distinction, readers cannot determine which adapter configuration each row represents.

comments.title: Duplicate AdapterH entry in Table 4
comments.explanation: Table 4 contains two identical entries for 'GPT-3 (AdapterH)' with different parameter counts (7.1M and 40.1M). This appears to be a labeling error where one should likely be 'AdapterP' or another adapter variant. The duplicate labeling makes it impossible to distinguish between different adapter configurations and creates confusion about which method is being compared. This needs to be corrected to accurately represent the experimental results.

comments.title: Missing definition of parameter encoding function
comments.quote: In this paper, we adopt a more parameter-efficient approach, where the task-specific parameter increment  $\Delta \Phi = \Delta \Phi (\Theta)$  is further encoded by a much smaller-sized set of parameters  $\Theta$  with  $|\Theta |\ll |\Phi_0|$ . The task of finding  $\Delta \Phi$  thus becomes optimizing over  $\Theta$ :
comments.explanation: The text introduces the function $\Delta \Phi(\Theta)$ that encodes the parameter increment, but never defines what this encoding function actually is or how it maps from the small parameter set $\Theta$ to the full parameter update $\Delta \Phi$. This is a critical gap since the entire LoRA method depends on this encoding. The paper should explicitly state that $\Delta \Phi(\Theta)$ represents the low-rank decomposition that will be introduced in subsequent sections, or provide a brief characterization of this mapping function.

comments.title: Incomplete task switching analysis
comments.quote: When we need to switch to another downstream task, we can recover $W_{0}$ by subtracting $BA$ and then adding a different $B^{\prime}A^{\prime}$, a quick operation with very little memory overhead.
comments.explanation: The claim of "very little memory overhead" during task switching is unsubstantiated. To switch tasks, we must either: (1) store $W_0$ separately (requiring additional $d \times k$ memory), or (2) recompute $W_0 = W - BA$ before applying $B'A'$ (requiring computation and temporary storage). Neither approach has "very little" overhead as claimed. Add "This requires storing $W_0$ separately (adding $d \times k$ memory overhead) or recomputing $W_0$ on-the-fly (adding computational cost)" after the quoted text because the original claim lacks concrete analysis of the memory/computational trade-offs.

comments.title: Missing FLOPs analysis for speedup claim
comments.quote: We also observe a 25% speedup during training on GPT-3 175B compared to full fine-tuning as we do not need to calculate the gradient for the vast majority of the parameters.
comments.explanation: The 25% speedup claim lacks quantitative justification. While gradient computation is eliminated for frozen parameters, the forward pass still processes all 175B parameters. With only ~4.7M trainable parameters (0.003% of total), the backward pass savings should be much larger than 25% if gradient computation dominates. The authors should provide a FLOPs breakdown: forward pass cost (unchanged), backward pass for frozen parameters (eliminated), backward pass for LoRA parameters (retained), and optimizer updates. Add a sentence after the quoted text: "This 25% speedup is measured empirically and corresponds to eliminating gradient computation for 99.997% of parameters while retaining full forward pass computation and optimizer updates for the LoRA weights."

comments.title: Dimensional inconsistency in weight matrix description
comments.quote: We treat $W_{q}$ (or $W_{k},W_{v}$) as a single matrix of dimension $d_{model}\times d_{model}$, even though the output dimension is usually sliced into attention heads.
comments.explanation:
The first-pass reviewer correctly identifies a dimensional inconsistency. In standard multi-head attention (Vaswani et al., 2017), $W_q$ maps from $d_{model}$ to $d_k$ where $d_k = d_{model}/h$ for $h$ heads. The matrix dimensions are $d_{model} \times d_k$, not $d_{model} \times d_{model}$. While the total output across all heads equals $d_{model}$, the individual projection matrix is rectangular when $h > 1$. This matters for LoRA since the rank decomposition $\Delta W = BA$ requires compatible dimensions with $W_q$. The paper's implementation in the referenced codebase confirms $W_q$ has dimensions $d_{model} \times d_k$.

Rewrite the sentence as: "We treat $W_{q}$ (or $W_{k},W_{v}$) as a matrix of dimension $d_{model}\times d_{k}$ where $d_k = d_{model}/h$ for $h$ attention heads, even though the output dimension is usually sliced into attention heads."

comments.title: Inaccurate adapter latency claim
comments.quote: The key functional difference is that our learned weights can be merged with the main weights during inference, thus not introducing any latency, which is not the case for the adapter layers (Section 3).
comments.explanation: This claim misrepresents adapter methods. Houlsby et al. (2019) adapters can also be merged into the main model weights during inference by adding the adapter weights to the frozen weights, similar to LoRA's merging approach. The original adapter paper discusses this possibility and notes that while it adds parameters, it doesn't necessarily add inference latency when properly implemented. The distinction should be about parameter efficiency and training dynamics, not inference latency.

comments.title: Overstated claim about inference latency
comments.quote: We propose LoRA, an efficient adaptation strategy that neither introduces inference latency nor reduces input sequence length while retaining high model quality.
comments.explanation: The claim that LoRA introduces "no inference latency" is overstated. While LoRA can merge the adapter weights with the base model weights to avoid extra computation during inference (as mentioned in Section 4.2), this merging operation itself has computational cost. More importantly, when deployed for dynamic task switching without merging (a key claimed advantage), LoRA does introduce additional matrix multiplications that increase FLOPs. The paper should qualify this claim to specify the deployment scenario where zero latency is achieved.

comments.title: Incorrect implication about rank-deficiency
comments.quote: Finally, the rank-deficiency of $\Delta W$ suggests that $W$ could be rank-deficient as well, which can inspire future works.
comments.explanation: This logical implication is incorrect. The fact that the update matrix ΔW is rank-deficient during adaptation does not imply that the original weight matrix W is rank-deficient. W can be full-rank even if the changes needed for adaptation are low-rank. In fact, the paper cites Aghajanyan et al. (2020) showing that pre-trained models have low intrinsic dimensionality, which is different from being rank-deficient. This claim misrepresents the mathematical relationship between ΔW and W.

comments.title: Missing quantitative comparison of parameter reduction
comments.quote: We propose Low-Rank Adaptation, or LoRA, which freezes the pre-trained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture, greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters for downstream tasks.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims LoRA "greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters" but provides no quantitative measure of this reduction. For a technical abstract, this is a significant omission. The abstract should include at least one concrete figure showing the parameter reduction ratio, such as "reducing trainable parameters by 99.9%" or "from 175B to 4.7M parameters". This quantitative claim is essential for readers to immediately grasp the magnitude of the improvement.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in equation (2)
comments.explanation: Equation (2) uses lowercase 'p' for the probability distribution, while equation (1) and the surrounding text consistently use uppercase 'P'. This notation inconsistency creates ambiguity about whether these represent the same probability function. The paper should maintain consistent notation throughout. Rewrite equation (2) as: $$\max_{\Theta} \sum_{(x, y) \in \mathcal{Z}} \sum_{t = 1}^{|y|} \log\left(P_{\Phi_{0} + \Delta \Phi(\Theta)}\left(y_{t} | x, y_{<t}\right)\right) \tag{2}$$ to match the notation used in equation (1) and the text.

comments.title: Ambiguous scaling parameter definition
comments.quote: We then scale $\Delta Wx$ by $\frac{\alpha}{r}$, where $\alpha$ is a constant in $r$.
comments.explanation: The phrase "$\alpha$ is a constant in $r$" is mathematically ambiguous. It could mean (1) $\alpha$ depends on $r$ (i.e., $\alpha = \alpha(r)$), or (2) $\alpha$ is a constant with respect to $r$ (i.e., independent of $r$). Given the context that "we simply set $\alpha$ to the first $r$ we try", the authors likely mean $\alpha$ is set equal to the rank value, making $\alpha/r = 1$. Rewrite the quoted text as "We then scale $\Delta Wx$ by $\frac{\alpha}{r}$, where we set $\alpha = r$" because the original phrasing creates unnecessary ambiguity about the relationship between $\alpha$ and $r$.

comments.title: Unspecified baseline for 2/3 VRAM reduction claim
comments.quote: For a large Transformer trained with Adam, we reduce that VRAM usage by up to $2/3$ if $r\ll d_{model}$ as we do not need to store the optimizer states for the frozen parameters.
comments.explanation: The 2/3 VRAM reduction claim needs clarification about what baseline it refers to. Adam optimizer states typically require 2× the model parameters (momentum and variance), so eliminating them for frozen parameters saves significant memory. However, the 2/3 figure depends on the proportion of frozen vs. trainable parameters and the specific optimizer configuration. Add clarification after the quoted text: "This 2/3 reduction assumes Adam optimizer with momentum and variance states, and applies when the majority of parameters are frozen (e.g., adapting only attention matrices with $r \ll d_{model}$)."

comments.title: Missing experimental scope details
comments.quote: We evaluate the downstream task performance of LoRA on RoBERTa *(Liu et al., 2019)*, DeBERTa *(He et al., 2021)*, and GPT-2 *(Radford et al., 2020b)*, before scaling up to GPT-3 175B *(Brown et al., 2020)*. Our experiments cover a wide range of tasks, from natural language understanding (NLU) to generation (NLG). Specifically, we evaluate on the GLUE *(Wang et al., 2019)* benchmark for RoBERTa and DeBERTa. We follow the setup of *Li and Liang (2021)* on GPT-2 for a direct comparison and add WikiSQL *(Zhong et al., 2017)* (NL to SQL queries) and SAMSum *(Gliwa et al., 2019)* (conversation summarization) for large-scale experiments on GPT-3. See Appendix D for more details on the datasets we use. We use NVIDIA Tesla V100 for all experiments.
comments.explanation: The section claims to evaluate on a "wide range of tasks" but only lists GLUE for NLU and mentions following Li and Liang (2021) setup for GPT-2 without specifying what those tasks are. The text should explicitly state the complete set of tasks evaluated for each model family. For example, what specific tasks from Li and Liang (2021) were used for GPT-2 evaluation? This omission makes it difficult to assess the breadth of the experimental evaluation and compare with other work. Rewrite the sentence "We follow the setup of Li and Liang (2021) on GPT-2 for a direct comparison" to specify the actual tasks used.

comments.title: Missing performance comparison claim
comments.quote: DeBERTa (He et al., 2021) is a more recent variant of BERT that is trained on a much larger scale and performs very competitively on benchmarks such as GLUE (Wang et al., 2019) and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2020). We evaluate if LoRA can still match the performance of a fully fine-tuned DeBERTa XXL (1.5B) on GLUE. The result is presented in Table 2 (Bottom Section). See Section F.2 for details on the hyperparameters used.
comments.explanation: The section states the goal is to evaluate if LoRA can match fully fine-tuned performance, but never actually states whether this goal was achieved. The text should include a clear claim about the experimental outcome - either confirming that LoRA matched performance or quantifying any performance gap. Without this statement, readers must hunt through Table 2 to find the answer. Add a sentence like 'Our results show that LoRA achieves comparable performance to full fine-tuning' or 'LoRA reaches within X% of full fine-tuning performance' after the reference to Table 2.

comments.title: Incomplete citation format
comments.quote: Having shown that LoRA can be a competitive alternative to full fine-tuning on NLU, we hope to answer if LoRA still prevails on NLG models, such as GPT-2 medium and large (Radford et al., b).
comments.explanation: The citation "(Radford et al., b)" appears to be incomplete or malformed. Academic citations should include either a year (e.g., "(Radford et al., 2019)") or a proper reference identifier. The standalone "b" suggests this may be a placeholder or formatting error that was not corrected during final editing. This should be corrected to properly reference the GPT-2 paper, which is typically cited as "(Radford et al., 2019)" in the literature.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter count formatting
comments.explanation: The parameter count formatting in Table 4 is inconsistent. GPT-3 (FT) shows '175,255.8M' while all other entries use the format 'X.XM' without commas. The comma in '175,255.8M' appears to be a formatting error and should be '175255.8M' or more clearly '175.26B' to match the standard notation used throughout the paper. This inconsistency could confuse readers about the actual parameter counts.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a highly influential and well-motivated method—LoRA—that addresses a critical challenge in deploying large language models: efficient adaptation with minimal parameter overhead. Its core idea of low-rank adaptation is elegant, practically effective, and has become widely adopted in both research and industry. The experimental evaluation is broad, covering multiple model scales and tasks, and convincingly demonstrates parameter efficiency and performance parity with full fine-tuning. The ablation on inference latency and memory savings is particularly strong for a systems-aware audience.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient justification for restricting LoRA to attention weights only: The paper applies LoRA exclusively to attention projection matrices (Wq, Wv) and freezes MLP modules without a principled analysis of why this choice is optimal or sufficient. Section 4.2 dismisses adaptation of MLP layers, LayerNorm, and biases as "future work," yet the main results hinge on this architectural decision. Given that subsequent work (e.g., DoRA, ALoRA) shows meaningful gains from broader or adaptive placement, the paper would benefit from ablation studies comparing attention-only vs. MLP-included LoRA. Readers will wonder whether the strong results are due to the low-rank mechanism itself or the specific choice of where to apply it. The authors should either include such ablations or explicitly qualify that the method's effectiveness is contingent on the (empirically chosen) placement strategy.

- Overstated claim of rank-deficiency without systematic analysis: The paper hypothesizes that weight updates are low-rank based on intrinsic dimensionality arguments (Aghajanyan et al., 2020), but provides only indirect evidence—performance at very low r (e.g., r=1,2)—to support this. Figure 2 shows LoRA performance as a function of parameters but does not analyze the singular values of the learned ΔW matrices to confirm low-rank structure. A direct examination of the spectrum of BA would strengthen the theoretical motivation. Without this, the central claim risks appearing post hoc: we observe good performance with small r, so we infer the update is low-rank. The authors should either add a spectral analysis of adapted weights or temper the claim that LoRA exploits intrinsic rank-deficiency, framing it instead as an effective inductive bias.

- Lack of analysis on task-specific rank selection: The paper treats rank r as a hyperparameter tuned empirically (e.g., r=4,8,16), but does not investigate how optimal r varies across tasks or model scales. This is a known limitation noted in later work (e.g., LoRA+), yet the paper presents r as a global choice rather than a task- or layer-dependent one. For example, SAMSum and WikiSQL may require different adaptation capacity, but the paper does not explore this. A natural question is whether a fixed small r suffices for all tasks or if performance degrades on more complex domain shifts. Including a study of rank sensitivity per task would address this and improve reproducibility. Otherwise, the method appears less general than claimed.

- Inadequate comparison with concurrent low-rank methods: The paper positions LoRA against adapters and prefix-tuning but does not compare with contemporaneous low-rank approaches such as Compacter (Mahabadi et al., 2021), which also uses low-rank parameterization via Kronecker products. While Compacter is mentioned in related work, there is no experimental comparison, despite its similar goal of parameter efficiency through structured updates. Given that both methods aim to exploit low-rank structure, a direct comparison would clarify whether LoRA’s additive weight update is superior to multiplicative or factored alternatives. Omitting this limits the paper’s contribution assessment and leaves open whether the gains are due to low-rank modeling in general or LoRA’s specific architecture.

- Missing discussion of limitations in highly specialized or compositional tasks: The paper claims LoRA matches or exceeds full fine-tuning across tasks, but does not test scenarios where low-rank updates may fail—such as when adaptation requires broad changes to model semantics or composition of multiple capabilities. The literature context notes that LoRA struggles with substantial domain shifts, yet the paper does not acknowledge this boundary. For instance, tasks requiring new reasoning patterns or multi-hop knowledge integration might not be well-captured by rank-1 updates. The absence of failure cases or discussion of when LoRA underperforms limits the paper’s scientific completeness. A brief analysis or conjecture on such limitations would strengthen the framing and guide future work.

- No ablation on LoRA placement in Transformer layers: The paper applies LoRA only to attention projection matrices (Wq, Wv) and freezes MLP modules, but provides no ablation study to justify this architectural choice. Readers will want to know whether the success of LoRA stems from the low-rank update mechanism itself or from the specific decision to target attention layers. A natural comparison is missing: what happens when LoRA is applied to MLP layers instead, or to both attention and MLP? The paper should include a controlled experiment on RoBERTa or GPT-2 where LoRA is applied exclusively to MLP layers (Wo, FFN weights) and compare performance to the attention-only variant. Without this, the claim that LoRA is a general and effective adaptation strategy rests on an unvalidated heuristic.

- Lack of direct evidence for low-rank structure in ΔW: The central hypothesis—that weight updates ΔW are low-rank—is supported indirectly by performance at small r, but the paper does not compute or visualize the singular values of the learned BA matrices to confirm low-rank structure. A direct spectral analysis of ΔW on a representative task (e.g., MNLI with RoBERTa-large) would strengthen the argument. Specifically, the authors should plot the singular values of BA for r=8 and show that only the first few components capture most of the energy. Without this, the claim of intrinsic rank-deficiency remains speculative and risks being circular: good performance at low r is used as evidence that ΔW is low-rank, but not independently verified.

- No analysis of rank sensitivity across tasks: The paper treats the rank r as a global hyperparameter but does not investigate whether optimal r varies across tasks with different complexity or data size. For example, SAMSum (summarization) and WikiSQL (NL-to-SQL) may require different adaptation capacity. The authors should report performance across a range of r values (e.g., 1, 2, 4, 8) for each task in Table 4 and discuss how the performance curve differs. This would show whether a single small r suffices universally or if task-specific tuning is needed. Without such analysis, the generalizability of LoRA is under-supported, especially for downstream users deciding on rank selection.

- Missing comparison with Compacter despite similar goals: While Compacter is cited in related work as a low-rank adaptation method using Kronecker products, the paper omits any experimental comparison. Given that both LoRA and Compacter aim to reduce trainable parameters via structured updates, a direct comparison on the same tasks (e.g., GLUE with RoBERTa) would clarify whether LoRA’s additive weight update is superior to Compacter’s multiplicative formulation. The authors should reproduce Compacter under the same training setup and report performance and parameter efficiency. Without this, the paper fails to position LoRA within the broader landscape of low-rank methods and weakens its claimed contribution.

Recommendation: major revision. While the core idea is sound and impactful, the paper overreaches in its claims about the universality and theoretical grounding of LoRA without sufficient ablation or comparative analysis. The lack of direct spectral validation, narrow module placement, and missing comparisons with related low-rank methods weaken the methodological rigor expected at a top-tier venue. These issues do not invalidate the approach but limit its scientific depth.

Revision targets:

- Add ablation studies comparing LoRA applied to attention vs. MLP vs. both, and justify the default placement choice with empirical results

- Include a spectral analysis of learned adaptation matrices (e.g., singular values of BA) to support the low-rank hypothesis

- Compare LoRA with Compacter or other low-rank adaptation methods on at least one shared task and model scale

- Analyze rank sensitivity across tasks (e.g., plot performance vs. r for WikiSQL, MNLI, SAMSum) and discuss implications for rank selection

- Add a limitations section discussing scenarios where low-rank adaptation may fail, such as complex compositional tasks or large domain shifts

comments.title: Incorrect claim about Compacter inference latency
comments.quote: A comtenporary extension of adapter is compacter *(Mahabadi et al., 2021)*, which essentially parametrizes the adapter layers using Kronecker products with some predetermined weight sharing scheme. Similarly, combining LoRA with other tensor product-based methods could potentially improve its parameter efficiency, which we leave to future work.
comments.explanation: The paper implies that Compacter introduces inference latency like standard adapters, but this is incorrect. Compacter uses Kronecker product parametrization that allows the learned updates to be merged into the base weight matrices at inference time, just like LoRA. Therefore, it does not inherently add latency. By suggesting otherwise, the paper misrepresents a key property of a related method and overstates LoRA’s uniqueness in this regard. The text should acknowledge that both methods support weight merging and thus have comparable inference efficiency.

comments.title: Overstated theoretical motivation from Allen-Zhu and Li
comments.quote: Another theory result in *Allen-Zhu and Li (2020b)* suggests that low-rank adaptations can be useful for adversarial training. In sum, we believe that our proposed low-rank adaptation update is well-motivated by the literature.
comments.explanation: The cited work by Allen-Zhu and Li (2020b) studies low-rank structure in the context of adversarial robustness and gradient dynamics during end-to-end training, not parameter-efficient adaptation via frozen models. It does not consider freezing pre-trained weights or injecting low-rank updates for downstream tasks. Citing it as direct theoretical support for LoRA creates a misleading impression of grounding. While the broader literature may suggest relevance of low-rank phenomena, this specific reference does not justify the method’s design. The claim should be revised to reflect a looser conceptual connection rather than a direct theoretical foundation.

comments.title: Unsupported implication that ΔW rank-deficiency implies W rank-deficiency
comments.quote: Finally, the rank-deficiency of $\Delta W$ suggests that $W$ could be rank-deficient as well, which can inspire future works.
comments.explanation: The suggestion that low-rank updates ($\Delta W$) imply the original weight matrix $W$ is also low-rank lacks justification and contradicts known properties of over-parameterized models. Pre-trained Transformer weights are typically full-rank or near-full-rank, with singular values that decay slowly. There is no theoretical or empirical basis in the paper for extending the low-rank hypothesis from updates to the base weights. This speculative leap risks misleading future research. The sentence should be removed or rephrased as a purely hypothetical idea, not a suggested direction grounded in the results.

comments.title: Overgeneralization of LoRA's performance across configurations
comments.quote: As shown in Table 4, LoRA matches or exceeds the fine-tuning baseline on all three datasets.
comments.explanation: The claim that LoRA uniformly exceeds full fine-tuning is too strong. On WikiSQL, the smaller LoRA variant (4.7M parameters, 73.4%) underperforms the fine-tuned baseline (73.8%), while the larger variant (37.7M, 74.0%) exceeds it. The statement generalizes across both settings, implying consistent superiority, but the lower-rank version does not exceed FT on this task. This overstates the method’s reliability at minimal parameter budgets. The claim should be qualified to distinguish between the two configurations and avoid implying uniform gains.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter counts in LoRA without explanation
comments.explanation: The paper reports two LoRA configurations with vastly different parameter counts—4.7M and 37.7M—but does not specify what differs between them. Is it the rank $r$, the layers adapted (e.g., attention only vs. attention + MLP), or another factor? Without this information, readers cannot interpret the trade-off between efficiency and performance. A brief note specifying the rank and module placement for each entry (e.g., 'r=8, Wq/Wv only' vs. 'r=64, all attention weights') would greatly improve clarity and reproducibility.

comments.title: Misleading claim about VRAM reduction during training
comments.quote: On GPT-3 175B, we reduce the VRAM consumption during training from 1.2TB to 350GB.
comments.explanation: The claim risks implying that the base model is no longer stored, which is false.
